annotations.label: Real interval

anchor.snippet: a real interval is the set of all real numbers lying between two fixed endpoints

annotations.note: Mathlib defines the four interval forms `Set.Icc`/`Ico`/`Ioc`/`Ioo` rather than a single 'real interval'; together with `Set.OrdConnected` they cover the notion.

annotations.label: Unit interval

anchor.snippet: the set of real numbers consisting of 0 , 1 , and all numbers in between is an interval

annotations.note: `unitInterval` is defined as `Set.Icc (0 : ℝ) 1`.

annotations.label: Interval (subset characterization)

anchor.snippet: An interval is a subset of the real numbers that contains all real numbers lying between any two numbers of the subset

annotations.note: `Set.OrdConnected s` says `[[x,y]] ⊆ s` whenever `x, y ∈ s`, matching the subset characterization.

annotations.label: Endpoints of an interval

anchor.snippet: The endpoints of an interval are its supremum (least upper bound), and its infimum (greatest lower bound)

annotations.note: No standalone 'endpoint' notion; sup/inf of an interval are computed lemma-by-lemma (e.g. `sSup_Icc`, `csInf_Ioo`).

annotations.label: Intervals determined by endpoints

anchor.snippet: Non-empty intervals are completely determined by their endpoints and whether each endpoint belongs to the interval

annotations.note: Endpoint-equality lemmas like `Icc_eq_Icc_iff` exist for each shape but there is no unified 'intervals are determined by endpoints' theorem.

annotations.label: Open interval

anchor.snippet: An open interval does not include any endpoint and can be succinctly indicated with parentheses

annotations.note: `Set.Ioo a b = {x | a < x ∧ x < b}` is exactly the open interval.

annotations.label: Open intervals as base of open sets

anchor.snippet: The open intervals are those intervals that are open sets for the usual topology on the real numbers, and they form a base of the open sets

annotations.note: `Real.isTopologicalBasis_Ioo_rat` shows rational-endpoint open intervals form a base; `isOpen_Ioo` gives that each is open.

annotations.label: Closed interval

anchor.snippet: A closed interval is an interval that includes all its finite endpoints

annotations.note: `Set.Icc a b = {x | a ≤ x ∧ x ≤ b}` is the closed interval.

annotations.label: Closed intervals as closed sets

anchor.snippet: The closed intervals are those intervals that are closed sets for the usual topology on the real numbers

annotations.note: `isClosed_Icc` proves `Set.Icc a b` is closed in any order-closed topology, including ℝ.

annotations.label: Half-open interval

anchor.snippet: A half-open interval has two distinct finite endpoints, and includes one but not the other

annotations.note: `Set.Ico` and `Set.Ioc` give the two half-open shapes.

annotations.label: Classification of intervals

anchor.snippet: a set of the real numbers is an interval, if and only if it is an open interval, a closed interval, or a half-open interval

annotations.note: Connected/OrdConnected subsets of ℝ are characterized as intervals, but there is no single lemma listing the eleven canonical shapes.

annotations.label: Degenerate interval

anchor.snippet: A degenerate interval is any set consisting of a single real number

annotations.note: `Set.Icc_self : Icc a a = {a}` realizes a degenerate closed interval but the term 'degenerate' is not introduced.

annotations.label: Proper interval

anchor.snippet: A real interval that is neither empty nor degenerate is said to be proper

annotations.note: No `Proper`/`Nontrivial` predicate on intervals is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Left/right-bounded interval

anchor.snippet: An interval is said to be left-bounded or right-bounded , if there is some real number that is, respectively, smaller than or larger than all its elements

annotations.note: `BddBelow` / `BddAbove` provide the left/right boundedness predicates for any set, including intervals.

annotations.label: Diameter/length of interval

anchor.snippet: Bounded intervals are bounded sets , in the sense that their diameter (which is equal to the absolute difference between the endpoints) is finite

annotations.note: `Real.diam_Icc` shows `Metric.diam (Icc a b) = b - a` when `a ≤ b`.

annotations.label: Centre and radius of interval

anchor.snippet: The centre ( midpoint ) of a bounded interval with endpoints a and b is ( a + b )/2 , and its radius is the half-length

annotations.note: `midpoint k a b = (a+b)/2` formalizes the centre; the interval radius is not a named concept though it equals `Metric.diam (Icc a b) / 2`.

annotations.label: Left/right-open by min/max

anchor.section: Categorisation by minimum and maximum elements
anchor.snippet: An interval is said to be left-open if and only if it contains no minimum

annotations.note: `IsLeast`/`IsGreatest` capture min/max-of-a-set, but Mathlib has no `LeftOpenInterval` predicate keyed to absence of a minimum.

annotations.label: Left/right-closed interval

anchor.section: Categorisation by minimum and maximum elements
anchor.snippet: An interval is said to be left-closed if it has a minimum element or is left-unbounded

annotations.note: No standalone 'left-closed interval' predicate exists; it would be expressed via `IsLeast` or `¬ BddBelow`.

annotations.label: Subinterval

anchor.snippet: An interval I is a subinterval of interval J if I is a subset of J

annotations.note: Subinterval = subset that is itself `OrdConnected`; no dedicated `Subinterval` predicate in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Interior and closure of interval

anchor.snippet: The interior of an interval I is the largest open interval that is contained in I

annotations.note: `interior_Icc`/`closure_Ioo` etc. compute interior and closure of each interval shape.

annotations.label: Interval enclosure

anchor.snippet: the interval enclosure or interval span of X is the unique interval that contains X

annotations.note: No `intervalHull`/`intervalSpan` operation is defined; the closest is the convex hull via `convexHull`.

annotations.label: Intervals are connected subsets

anchor.snippet: The intervals are precisely the connected subsets of

annotations.note: `isPreconnected_iff_ordConnected` (and `setOf_isPreconnected_eq_of_ordered`) characterize connected subsets of a conditionally complete linear order as the OrdConnected (= interval) ones.

annotations.label: Intervals are convex subsets

anchor.snippet: The intervals are also the convex subsets of

annotations.note: `convex_iff_ordConnected` shows convex subsets of an ordered field coincide with OrdConnected (interval) subsets.

annotations.label: Closure of an interval

anchor.snippet: The closure of an interval is the union of the interval and the set of its finite endpoints, and hence is also an interval

annotations.note: `closure_Ioo`, `closure_Ico`, `closure_Ioc` give `Icc a b` as the closure, matching the union with endpoints.

annotations.label: Intersection and union of intervals

anchor.snippet: The intersection of any collection of intervals is always an interval

annotations.note: `OrdConnected.inter` (and `ordConnected_iInter`) show arbitrary intersections of intervals are intervals.

annotations.label: Open and closed balls on the real line

anchor.snippet: its open balls are the open bounded intervals

annotations.note: `Real.ball_eq_Ioo` identifies open metric balls in ℝ with bounded open intervals.

annotations.label: Trichotomy partition

anchor.snippet: Any element x of an interval I defines a partition of I into three disjoint intervals

annotations.note: Lemmas like `Iio_union_Ioi` plus singletons assemble the trichotomy, but there is no single 'trichotomy of an interval at x' lemma.

annotations.label: Dyadic interval

anchor.snippet: A dyadic interval is a bounded real interval whose endpoints are

annotations.note: Mathlib has no `DyadicInterval` definition.

annotations.label: Properties of dyadic intervals

anchor.snippet: Dyadic intervals have the following properties

annotations.note: Without a dyadic-interval definition, no properties of them are formalized.

annotations.label: Continuity (epsilon-delta)

anchor.snippet: is said to be continuous at a point

annotations.note: `Continuous` is defined topologically; `Metric.continuous_iff` gives the ε–δ characterization on metric spaces, specializing to ℝ.

annotations.label: Intermediate value theorem

anchor.snippet: The intermediate value theorem captures the intuition

annotations.note: `intermediate_value_Icc` is the IVT for continuous functions on `[a, b]`.

annotations.label: Confidence interval

anchor.snippet: a confidence interval is evaluated from a random sample and the interval endpoints are real-valued random variables

annotations.note: No `ConfidenceInterval` definition exists in Mathlib's probability/statistics layer.

annotations.label: Tychonoff embedding into product of intervals

anchor.snippet: Every Tychonoff space is embeddable into a product space of the closed unit intervals

annotations.note: Mathlib has `CompletelyRegularSpace` and unit-interval Tietze-style results, but no general 'Tychonoff space embeds into a cube `[0,1]^I`' theorem.

annotations.label: Order topology is completely normal

anchor.snippet: every totally ordered set endowed with the order topology is completely normal

annotations.note: `OrderTopology.completelyNormalSpace` (and `t5Space`) prove a linearly ordered space with the order topology is completely normal. (The article also asserts the stronger 'monotonically normal' result, which is not in Mathlib.)

annotations.label: Open/closed ball as interval

anchor.snippet: An open finite interval

annotations.note: `Real.ball_eq_Ioo` (and `Real.closedBall_eq_Icc`) identify ℝ-balls with intervals.

annotations.label: Multi-dimensional interval

anchor.snippet: an axis-aligned hyperrectangle (or box) is the Cartesian product

annotations.note: `BoxIntegral.Box ι` is an axis-aligned box; its Icc is `Set.pi univ (fun i ↦ Icc (lower i) (upper i))`.

annotations.label: Facets, faces, and corners

anchor.snippet: A facet of such an interval

annotations.note: `BoxIntegral.Box.face` defines a hyperface of a box; general facets/corners of arbitrary-dimension boxes are not separately defined.

annotations.label: Convex polytope as intersection of half-spaces

anchor.snippet: Any finite interval can be constructed as the intersection of half-bounded intervals

annotations.note: `Set.Icc a b = Set.Ici a ∩ Set.Iic b` (and analogues for boxes) realize the half-space intersection, but no general convex-polytope/H-representation theorem is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Domain

anchor.snippet: a non-empty connected open set is called a domain

annotations.note: No `Domain` (= nonempty connected open set) abbreviation is defined in Mathlib; one writes the conjunction directly.

annotations.label: Complex interval

anchor.snippet: Intervals of complex numbers can be defined as regions of the complex plane

annotations.note: Mathlib does not introduce a complex-interval/rectangle type beyond products of real intervals via `Set.reProdIm` for measures.

annotations.label: Interval in preordered set

anchor.snippet: The concept of intervals can be defined in arbitrary partially ordered sets or more generally, in arbitrary preordered sets

annotations.note: `Set.Icc`, `Ico`, `Ioc`, `Ioo`, `Ici`, `Iic`, `Ioi`, `Iio` are defined for `Preorder α` directly.

annotations.label: Order-convex subset

anchor.section: Convex sets and convex components in order theory
anchor.snippet: is (order-)convex if for every

annotations.note: `Set.OrdConnected` is precisely the order-convexity predicate.

annotations.label: Convex but non-interval in rationals

anchor.section: Convex sets and convex components in order theory
anchor.snippet: in the totally ordered set

annotations.note: No explicit Mathlib example formalizes 'OrdConnected ⊊ interval-set' in ℚ; `Rat`-flavored disconnection facts exist (`Mathlib.Topology.Instances.RatLemmas`) but not as this example.

annotations.label: Convex component

anchor.section: Convex sets and convex components in order theory
anchor.snippet: A maximal element of this poset is called a convex component

annotations.note: `Set.ordConnectedComponent s x` is the maximal OrdConnected subset of `s` through `x`.

annotations.label: Convex components form a partition

anchor.section: Convex sets and convex components in order theory
anchor.snippet: the convex components of a subset of a totally ordered set form a partition

annotations.note: Mathlib has `ordConnectedComponent` and basic membership lemmas but no theorem packaging them as a partition (`Setoid`/`IsPartition`) of the ambient set.

annotations.label: Characterizations of intervals in a linear continuum

anchor.snippet: A generalization of the characterizations of the real intervals follows

annotations.note: The OrdConnected ↔ preconnected equivalence covers the linear-continuum characterization; finer 'eleven shapes' classification is absent.

annotations.label: Sublattice and convex set characterization

anchor.snippet: For a subset

annotations.note: No theorem characterizing intervals as convex sublattices appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Topological ring of intervals

anchor.snippet: the collection of all intervals

annotations.note: `NonemptyInterval` carries algebraic instances but Mathlib has no topological ring of `(a, b)` ↦ `Icc a b` on ℝ × ℝ matching this construction.

annotations.label: Ideals and units of interval algebra

anchor.snippet: The direct sum algebra

annotations.note: The ideal/unit structure of the topological interval algebra is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Isomorphism with hyperbolic numbers

anchor.snippet: the ring of intervals has been identified

annotations.note: Mathlib has no `HyperbolicNumber`/`SplitComplex` type, so the claimed ring isomorphism is not formalized.

annotations.label: Integer interval

anchor.snippet: used to indicate the interval of all integers between a and b included

annotations.note: `Finset.Icc a b` (and `Ico`, `Ioc`, `Ioo`) on `ℤ` (via `LocallyFiniteOrder`) gives the finite integer interval; `Set.Icc` on `ℤ` is the set-level analogue.

annotations.label: Image of an interval under a continuous function is an interval

anchor.snippet: An equivalent formulation of the theorem asserts that the image of an interval by a continuous function is an interval

annotations.note: Combining `IsPreconnected.image` with `isPreconnected_iff_ordConnected` yields that a continuous image of a real interval is preconnected, hence an interval.

annotations.label: Extended-real interval

anchor.snippet: an interval may be defined as a subset of the extended real numbers

annotations.note: `Set.Icc`, `Ico`, `Ioc`, `Ioo` instantiated at `EReal` provide intervals on the extended reals, including `[−∞, +∞]`.
